deity.summary: Complete bundleable deity/temple dataset for the DEITY/GOD/TEMPLE spiritual layer. All five datasets are static lookups (no birth-time needed), key off already-computed nakshatra/weekday/tithi/graha, and use small tables (7/9/15/27 rows). Anchor sources are Wikipedia (List_of_Nakshatras, List_of_Natchathara_temples, Navagraha_temples_in_Tamil_Nadu) — verified, reusable text; deity STORIES are 1-2 sentence retellings (facts, not copyrightable). Beej mantras are traditional public-domain Sanskrit. Positioning stays as respectful cultural garnish: temple names, deity stories, traditional vrat — never prediction. KEY BEATS: (a) "Your nakshatra's guardian deity" card with story + which real Tamil Nadu temple honors it; (b) "Your weekday god" — day-of-week vrat/deity/graha; (c) Navagraha temple map (9 real temples near Kumbakonam) tied to user's rashi-lord/nakshatra-lord graha; (d) tithi deity on the birth tithi. Images: Wikimedia has PD/CC photos of every navagraha temple and most nakshatra temples, plus classic Ravi Varma PD deity paintings (Lakshmi, Saraswati, Hanuman, Shiva, Surya). VERIFIED GAPS: individual per-temple presiding deity (Shiva vs Vishnu form) for the 27 nakshatra temples is partial on Wikipedia — temple NAMES are solid, but if a "deity of the nakshatra temple" field is wanted it needs a second-pass per-temple lookup. Nakshatra deity + ruling planet, weekday deity/graha/vrat, navagraha deity+temple+beej mantra, and tithi deity are all fully verified.

ideas.title: Your Nakshatra's Guardian Deity (+ story + real temple)
ideas.what: From the already-computed birth nakshatra (1 of 27), show the presiding Vedic deity, a 1-2 line origin story, and the specific Tamil Nadu 'birth-star temple' that honors it. Keys purely off nakshatra index — pure static lookup, no birth time. E.g. Ashwini -> Ashvini Kumaras (twin celestial physicians, sons of Surya, healers who revived the dead) -> Thiruthuraipoondi Piravi Marundeeswarar Temple.
ideas.source: Wikipedia List_of_Nakshatras (deity+planet, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Nakshatras) + Wikipedia List_of_Natchathara_temples (27 temple names+towns, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Natchathara_temples). Both static tables, bundle at build. Stories are short factual retellings from Vedic myth (not copyrightable).
ideas.feasible: easy; birth-time-free (nakshatra already computed). Deity+planet+temple-name all verified; only per-temple presiding-deity detail is a soft gap.
ideas.wow: Deeply personal 'this is YOUR god and here is the actual temple in India you could visit' — highest emotional pull, keys off a number they already have.

ideas.title: Your Weekday God (vara -> deity + graha + vrat)
ideas.what: From the birthday's weekday (already derivable from date), show the ruling deity, ruling planet, and the traditional fast/vrat for that day. E.g. born on a Tuesday -> Mangalvar -> Hanuman + Mangal (Mars) -> Mangalvar vrat for courage/strength.
ideas.source: hindutone.com Hindu days-of-week + pujayagna.com vrat pages (Somvar/Mangalvar/Budhwar/Guruvar/Shukravar/Shanivar/Ravivar). 7-row static table. Weekday-from-date is trivial JS (Zeller/Date).
ideas.feasible: trivial; birth-time-free. Fully verified across sources.
ideas.wow: Everyone knows their birth weekday; instantly ties them to a familiar god (Hanuman/Shiva/Lakshmi/Shani) + a fast their family probably keeps.

ideas.title: Navagraha Temple for Your Ruling Planet
ideas.what: Take the graha that rules the user's nakshatra (or rashi lord — already computed), and point to its real Navagraha temple in the Kumbakonam circuit, with the temple's presiding deity + beej mantra. E.g. planet Shani -> Dharbaranyeswarar Temple, Thirunallar (the famous Saturn temple) + beej 'Aum Pram Prim Pron Sah Shanicharaye Namah'.
ideas.source: Wikipedia Navagraha_temples_in_Tamil_Nadu (temple+deity+town, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Navagraha_temples_in_Tamil_Nadu) + astrotalk.com/drikpanchang beej mantras. 9-row static table. Only 7 classical grahas map to temples (Rahu/Ketu included = 9); works off nakshatra-lord which is one of the 9.
ideas.wow: Turns the abstract 'your planet' into a pin on a real map of 9 famous temples — plus a chantable one-line mantra.

ideas.title: Your Birth-Tithi Deity
ideas.what: From the already-computed tithi (1-15), show the tithi's presiding deity. E.g. born on Chaturthi -> Ganesha; Ekadashi -> Vishnu; Purnima -> Chandra; Amavasya -> Pitrs (ancestors).
ideas.source: clickastro.com tithi list + jyotishguide.com panchang tithi (15-row static table). Tithi already computed in the app.
ideas.wow: Adds a third independent 'divine coordinate' (day-deity) alongside nakshatra-deity and weekday-deity, so almost every user gets a rich 3-deity combo.

ideas.title: Ishta Devata / 'Your personal deity' summary card
ideas.what: Synthesis card combining nakshatra deity + weekday deity + tithi deity into a small 'your spiritual signature' panel, framed explicitly as cultural tradition (Ishta Devata = one's cherished chosen deity concept), never prediction.
ideas.source: Composes the four tables above; Ishta Devata concept from astrosight.ai/AstroVed. No new data beyond the 4 bundled tables.
ideas.wow: Ties the whole spiritual layer into one shareable summary — the 'kundli' emotional payoff without any fortune-telling claim.

tables.name: nakshatra_deity
tables.sample: Ashwini | Ashvins (twin celestial physicians, sons of Surya & Sanjna-as-mare; healers who revived the dead & restored youth) | Ketu || Krittika | Agni (fire god, purifier, carrier of offerings, cosmic priest) | Surya || Rohini | Brahma/Prajapati (creator; nakshatra of fertility & abundance) | Chandra || Anuradha | Mitra (god of friendship & divine contracts) | Shani || Revati | Pushan (nourisher-protector, guardian of travelers & herds, an Aditya) | Budha

tables.name: weekday_deity
tables.sample: Sunday/Ravivar | Surya (Sun God) | Surya/Sun | Ravivar vrat — health, vitality, leadership || Monday/Somvar | Shiva (& Chandra) | Chandra/Moon | Somvar vrat — peace, wish-fulfilment || Tuesday/Mangalvar | Hanuman (& Mangal) | Mangal/Mars | Mangalvar vrat — courage, strength, protection || Saturday/Shanivar | Shani (& Hanuman) | Shani/Saturn | Shanivar vrat — relief from Shani dosha/karmic delays

tables.name: navagraha_temple
tables.sample: Surya/Sun | Suryanar Kovil, Suryanar Kovil (near Kumbakonam) | Aum Hraam Hreem Hraum Sah Suryaya Namah || Shani/Saturn | Dharbaranyeswarar Temple, Thirunallar (Karaikal) | Aum Pram Prim Pron Sah Shanicharaye Namah || Guru/Jupiter | Apatsahayesvarar Temple, Alangudi | Om Brim Brihaspataye Namah || Rahu | Naganathar Temple, Tirunageswaram | Om Bhraam Bhreem Bhraum Sah Rahave Namah || Ketu | Nagannathaswamy Temple, Keelaperumpallam | Aum Sraam Sreem Sraum Sah Ketave Namah

tables.name: nakshatra_temple_tamilnadu
tables.sample: Ashwini | Thiruthuraipoondi Piravi Marundeeswarar Temple | Thiruthuraipoondi || Bharani | Nalladai Agneeswarar Temple | Nalladai || Rohini | Kancheepuram Sri Pandava Dhootha Perumal Temple | Kancheepuram || Swathi | Chithukadu Dhaathreeswarar Temple | Siddharkadu (Chennai) || Revathi | Kargudi Kailasanathar Temple | Kargudi

tables.name: tithi_deity
tables.sample: Pratipada (1) | Agni || Chaturthi (4) | Ganesha (& Yama) || Ekadashi (11) | Vishnu || Chaturdashi (14) | Shiva || Purnima (15) | Chandra; Amavasya | Pitrs (ancestors)

regional.summary: Complete state-keyed data for the REGIONAL/NATIVE identity angle, all from free/reusable sources (Wikipedia CC-BY-SA text + PD/CC Wikimedia images). Five bundleable static tables, each ~28 states + major UTs, plus calendar-era conversion formulas that turn birth YEAR into the user's regional era-year and Tamil cycle-name — no birth time needed, pure date+state computation. VERIFIED anchors: Malayalam/Kollam Era = Gregorian−825 (−824 before ~Aug 17); Bengali San = Gregorian−594 (−593 after ~Apr 15); Vikram Samvat = Gregorian+57 (+56 Jan–Mar); Shaka = Gregorian−78 (−79 Jan–Mar); Tamil 60-yr cycle index = ((Y−1987) mod 60)+1 with year 1 = Prabhava, so 1985 → Krodhana (matches brief), 1960 → Raudri, etc. Odia Amli ≈ Gregorian−592/593. Regional new years cluster: lunisolar Chaitra (Ugadi/Gudi Padwa/Cheti Chand/Navreh) late-Mar–early-Apr; solar Mesha Sankranti Apr 13–15 (Baisakhi/Vishu/Puthandu/Bihu/Pana Sankranti/Pohela Boishakh); Gujarat Bestu Varas at Diwali (Oct–Nov); Sikkim Losoong (Dec). Every state also has an official animal/bird/flower/tree (Wikipedia "List of Indian state ___" pages — authoritative, complete for 28 states + UTs), a signature art form, folk dance, and dish. All five tables are small (<40 rows), precompute-friendly, and key off the birth-STATE the site already derives from the city. New-year and era-year additionally key off the birth MONTH (to pick the right offset branch) and YEAR.

ideas.title: Your people's New Year is X, around <month>
ideas.what: Given birth STATE, show the regional/community New Year: name, native script, the Gregorian month it lands in, and calendar type (lunisolar Chaitra vs solar Mesha Sankranti). e.g. Maharashtra→Gudi Padwa (late Mar/early Apr, lunisolar), Kerala→Vishu (Apr 14, solar), Gujarat→Bestu Varas (day after Diwali, Oct–Nov). Static state→festival map; no per-year date needed if we say 'around <month>', but we CAN compute the exact date the same way the site already computes tithi/masa (Chaitra Shukla Pratipada for lunisolar; Mesha Sankranti for solar).
ideas.source: Wikipedia 'Indian New Year's days' (CC-BY-SA) https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indian_New_Year's_days ; per-festival Wikipedia pages (Gudi Padwa, Vishu, Puthandu, Vaisakhi, Pohela Boishakh, Bihu, Ugadi, Cheti Chand, Bestu Varas). Cross-checked with ClearIAS/Prokerala festival guides.
ideas.feasible: trivial — birth-time-free. Static state→{name, nativeName, monthWindow, calendarType} lookup, ~15 distinct festivals mapped to 28 states. Exact date optional (reuse existing sidereal engine for Chaitra-Pratipada / Mesha-Sankranti).
ideas.wow: 'Your people ring in the new year not on 1 January but on <date> — it's called <Vishu>, and this year it falls around mid-April.' Ties the user's identity to a living tradition most Indians know but rarely see named for THEIR community.

ideas.title: Your birth year in your own calendar era
ideas.what: Convert the Gregorian birth YEAR into the regional era-year for the birth region: Malayalam/Kollam Era (Kerala), Bengali San (WB/Assam), Vikram Samvat (North/Gujarat/Rajasthan), Shaka (national/Deccan), Tamil 60-name cycle year (TN), Odia Amli (Odisha). e.g. 'Your birth year 1985 = Kollam 1160 / Tamil year Krodhana / Bengali 1392 / Vikram Samvat 2042.' Keys off state→era + a fixed subtraction/offset, plus birth MONTH to pick the pre/post-new-year branch.
ideas.source: Malayalam calendar https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malayalam_calendar (ME = Gregorian−825/−824); Bengali calendar https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bengali_calendar (San = −594/−593); Vikram Samvat https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vikram_Samvat (+57/+56); Saka era (−78/−79); Tamil calendar https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tamil_calendar + srirangaminfo 60-name list (anchor 1987=Prabhava); Odia/Anka year https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anka_year (~−592). All CC-BY-SA / public-domain factual offsets.
ideas.feasible: easy — pure integer arithmetic, birth-time-free. Formulas: ME=Y−825; San=Y−594; VS=Y+57; Shaka=Y−78 (each −1 if birth month is before that era's new-year month). Tamil: idx=((Y−1987) mod 60)+1 → name from 60-entry array. One small state→era-list table + one 60-name array.
ideas.wow: Seeing '1985 = Krodhana' or 'Kollam 1160' reframes a boring Gregorian year as the year your grandparents actually wrote on letters and horoscopes. High shareability, uniquely 'yours'.

ideas.title: Your state's official emblems (animal / bird / flower / tree)
ideas.what: Given birth state, show the 4 official state symbols as a little identity crest: state animal, bird, flower, tree — with a Wikimedia PD/CC image each. e.g. Rajasthan→Chinkara / Great Indian bustard / Rohira / Khejri; West Bengal→Fishing cat / White-throated kingfisher / Night-flowering jasmine / Devil's tree. Pure static state→{animal,bird,flower,tree} lookup.
ideas.source: Wikipedia 'List of Indian state animals' / '…state birds' / '…state trees' / '…state flowers' (all CC-BY-SA, complete for 28 states + UTs). Images: Wikimedia Commons — most species photos are CC-BY-SA / CC0; pick PD or CC-BY ones and store attribution.
ideas.feasible: easy — birth-time-free static lookup. 4 columns × ~36 rows. Only real work is curating one legally-clean Wikimedia image per unique species (many species repeat across states, so ~60–70 images total).
ideas.wow: A tiny 'coat of arms' for the user's home state — most people don't know their state bird/flower. Visual, screenshot-friendly, deepens the regional-identity theme the site already leans into.

ideas.title: Your state's signature art form
ideas.what: Given birth state, name the signature folk/painting tradition with one representative Wikimedia image: Bihar→Madhubani, Maharashtra→Warli, Odisha→Pattachitra, Rajasthan→Phad, West Bengal→Kalighat, Andhra→Kalamkari, Kerala→Mural, Karnataka→Mysore painting, Tamil Nadu→Tanjore, Punjab→Phulkari, Uttarakhand→Aipan, MP→Gond. Static state→artForm map.
ideas.source: Wikipedia per-art pages (Madhubani art, Warli, Pattachitra, Phad painting, Kalighat painting, Kalamkari, Kerala mural, Mysore painting, Tanjore painting, Phulkari, Gond art) — CC-BY-SA. Overview: MeMeraki 'state-wise map of Indian folk art', TheBetterIndia GI-tag folk-art map. Images: Wikimedia Commons CC/PD.
ideas.feasible: easy — birth-time-free static lookup, ~28 rows. Image curation is the only effort; many art pages have CC-licensed sample images on Commons.
ideas.wow: 'The walls of a home in your region are painted in <Warli> — here's what it looks like.' Turns an abstract 'where you're from' into a concrete visual craft, very shareable and rarely surfaced per-user.

ideas.title: Your state's folk dance + signature dish
ideas.what: Two more identity garnishes per state: the iconic folk dance (Punjab→Bhangra, Gujarat→Garba, Rajasthan→Ghoomar, Assam→Bihu, Maharashtra→Lavani, Karnataka→Yakshagana, Kerala→Theyyam, Manipur→Lai Haraoba) and the signature dish (Bihar→Litti Chokha, Gujarat→Dhokla, J&K→Rogan Josh, Tamil Nadu→Pongal, Maharashtra→Puran Poli, WB→Macher Jhol, Punjab→Sarson da Saag). Two static state→X maps.
ideas.source: Wikipedia 'List of Indian folk dances' (CC-BY-SA) https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Indian_folk_dances ; dish sources: Holidify '29 traditional foods by state', per-dish Wikipedia pages (Litti, Puran poli, Rogan josh, Macher jhol, Dhokla, Pongal). Images optional from Wikimedia Commons CC/PD.
ideas.feasible: trivial — birth-time-free, two ~28-row text lookups. Dance and dish can be text-only (emoji garnish) or add one Commons image each.
ideas.wow: Rounds out a mini 'passport of your homeland': your new year, your era-year, your state crest, your art, your dance, your dish — a complete regional-identity card generated from just a city + date. Strong reason to share the whole card.

tables.name: stateNewYear
tables.keyedBy: state (28 states + major UTs, ~15 distinct festivals)
tables.sample: ["Maharashtra","Gudi Padwa","गुढी पाडवा","Mar–Apr (Chaitra Shukla 1)","lunisolar"] | ["Karnataka","Ugadi","ಯುಗಾದಿ","Mar–Apr (Chaitra Shukla 1)","lunisolar"] | ["Kerala","Vishu","വിഷു","Apr 14–15 (Mesha Sankranti)","solar"] | ["Tamil Nadu","Puthandu","புத்தாண்டு","Apr 14 (Mesha Sankranti)","solar"] | ["West Bengal","Pohela Boishakh","পহেলা বৈশাখ","Apr 14–15","solar"] | ["Punjab","Baisakhi","ਵਿਸਾਖੀ","Apr 13–14","solar"] | ["Assam","Bohag/Rongali Bihu","ৰঙালী বিহু","Apr 14–15","solar"] | ["Gujarat","Bestu Varas","બેસતું વર્ષ","Oct–Nov (day after Diwali)","lunisolar"] | ["Odisha","Pana Sankranti (Maha Bishuba)","ପଣା ସଂକ୍ରାନ୍ତି","Apr 13–14","solar"]

tables.keyedBy: state → era name + offset formula (+ shared 60-name Tamil array)
tables.rowCount: ~10 era rows + 60-name array; mapped onto 28 states

festival.summary: PANCHANG/FESTIVALS angle for Janam Express — all verified against the project's own engine. KEY FINDING: the app already bundles `mhah-panchang`, whose `calendar(date,lat,lon)` and `calculate(date)` return Yoga, Karna AND Ritu objects (each with name_en_IN + ino index) — the app currently reads only Tithi/Nakshatra/Paksha/Masa and throws the rest away (astronomy.js lines 46-63). So YOGA, KARANA and RITU are already computed at runtime and cost ZERO new dependency: just read cal.Yoga/cal.Karna/cal.Ritu and join to a tiny bundled meaning table keyed by the engine's exact ino index (0-based). I extracted the engine's EXACT output-string arrays from node_modules so the meaning tables key correctly (the engine uses idiosyncratic spellings: Yoga 'Prithi'/'Vaidhruthi', Karna 'Bawa'/'Sakuna'/'Kimstughana', Tithi 'Chavithi'/'Punnami', Masa starts at index0='Baisakha'). AYANA (Uttarayana/Dakshinayana) is a trivial pure function of the Sun's sidereal longitude the kundli engine already computes (Makara 270°→Mithuna 90° = Uttarayana). TITHI-BIRTHDAY next-occurrence is computable by scanning forward day-by-day for the matching (masa,paksha,tithi) using the same engine (~365 calls, fine at load or on click). FESTIVALS: two viable routes — (A) compute each festival by scanning a year for its fixed (masa,paksha,tithi) rule via the engine [no bundle, but risk on adhika-masa & purnimanta/amanta & drik-vs-suryasiddhanta edge cases → can be off by a day/month for some years]; (B) bundle a verified static table 1950-2010 (~10 festivals x 61 yrs = 610 rows, ~8KB) scraped from epicastrology.com (confirmed to hold full 1950-2050 per-festival Gregorian date lists) or drikpanchang year-archive. RECOMMENDATION: bundle the static table for the ~10 headline festivals (accuracy guaranteed, matches the app's 'precompute data' ethos) and compute yoga/karana/ritu/ayana/tithi-birthday live. Special-tithi births and 'your star's festival' and 'how many Diwalis' all fall out of data already present.

ideas.title: Complete the 5-limb Panchang: add Yoga + Karana (already computed, zero cost)
ideas.what: The app shows Tithi/Nakshatra/Paksha/Masa but panchang has 5 limbs (vaara/tithi/nakshatra/yoga/karana). Add the two missing: YOGA (Sun+Moon combined longitude / 13°20', 27 values) and KARANA (half-tithi, 6° of elongation, 11 recurring names). Show engine's name + a one-line meaning/nature from a bundled 27-row and 11-row table keyed by the engine's integer ino index. Keys off computed date only (no birth time needed — uses the same ~6AM-IST sample instant already used for tithi).
ideas.source: ALREADY IN THE BUNDLE. astronomy.js already imports mhah-panchang and calls p.calendar(date,lat,lon) which returns cal.Yoga and cal.Karna objects {name, name_en_IN, ino, start, end} — currently discarded. Just add `yoga: cal.Yoga` / `karana: cal.Karna` to the returned object (astronomy.js:52-58). Meaning table is a static lookup I compiled from https://www.astrogle.com/astrology/27-yogas-11-karanas-panchangam.html and https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karana_(pancanga). Verified engine name arrays extracted from node_modules/mhah-panchang/dist/mhah-panchang.esm.js:826-833.
ideas.feasible: TRIVIAL. Birth-time-free (uses existing near-sunrise sample). No new dependency, no network, ~1KB of added JSON. The only care needed: the meaning table must key by ino index (0-based) not by name-guess, because the engine's spellings are idiosyncratic ('Prithi','Vaidhruthi','Bawa','Sakuna','Kimstughana').
ideas.wow: Completes the panchang to all five limbs like a real almanac page — 'On the morning you were born the sky read: tithi X, nakshatra Y, yoga Harshana (joy/thrill), karana Bava (a good-omen half-day for beginnings).' Feels like a printed panchang from the year of birth.

ideas.title: Tithi-Birthday: your REAL Hindu birthday and when it next falls
ideas.what: Explain that traditionally Hindus celebrate the anniversary of their birth TITHI within its MASA+PAKSHA, not the Gregorian date — so the 'real' birthday drifts ~11 days earlier each year and lands on a different English date annually. Then compute + show the NEXT occurrence from today (2026): scan forward day-by-day from today, evaluating the engine's (masa,paksha,tithi) each day, return the first date whose triple matches the birth triple. Keys off the computed birth (masa,paksha,tithi) which the app already has.
ideas.source: Concept + method verified: https://www.janmatithi.in/what-is-janma-tithi-and-how-to-find-yours/ and https://www.drikpanchang.com/utilities/tithi/hindu-shraddha-tithi-calculator.html (Tithi Pravesh = Sun-Moon return to same relative angle). Computation reuses the already-bundled mhah-panchang engine — no new source.
ideas.feasible: EASY-MEDIUM. Birth-time-free (tithi already sampled at ~sunrise). Implementation is a forward scan of up to ~385 days calling engine.calendar() once per day; each call is cheap (pure math, no network). Do it lazily on click, not on initial load, to keep cheap-Android snappy. Edge case to handle: adhika-masa years can make a tithi occur twice or 'skip' — acceptable to return the nearest match and footnote it.
ideas.wow: 'Your Gregorian birthday is 12 August. But your janma-tithi — Bhadrapada Krishna Ashtami, the same tithi as Krishna himself — next falls on 2 Sept 2026. That's the day your grandmother would have marked.' Deeply resonant, and almost nobody knows their own tithi-birthday date.

ideas.title: Special-tithi births: you share a tithi with a god or a vrat
ideas.what: If the birth tithi is one of the 'named' special tithis, surface its significance: Purnima (full-moon, Guru/Hanuman births, completeness), Amavasya (new-moon; Diwali/Lakshmi if Kartika, ancestor/pitru energy), Ekadashi (Vishnu fasting day, x2/month), Chaturthi (Ganesh — Bhadrapada Shukla Chaturthi = Ganesh Chaturthi), Ashtami (Shakti/Kala-Bhairava; Bhadrapada Krishna Ashtami = Janmashtami/Krishna), Navami (Chaitra Shukla Navami = Ram Navami), Chaturdashi (Krishna Chaturdashi of Phalguna = Maha Shivratri). Show 'you were born on the same tithi as Lord Krishna's birth' when masa+paksha also match.
ideas.source: Bundled 15-tithi->deity/festival table (see tables). Mappings verified: Ganesh=Bhadrapada Shukla Chaturthi (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ganesh_Chaturthi), Janmashtami=Bhadrapada Krishna Ashtami, Ram Navami=Chaitra Shukla Navami, Maha Shivratri=Phalguna Krishna Chaturdashi (https://www.drikpanchang.com/hindu-festivals/maha-shivaratri/maha-shivaratri.html), Ekadashi=Vishnu (https://www.drikpanchang.com/vrats/ekadashidates.html).
ideas.feasible: TRIVIAL. Birth-time-free. Pure lookup on the tithi index (and optionally masa+paksha for the exact-festival match) the app already computes. ~15-row static table, <1KB.
ideas.wow: 'You were born on Ashtami — the tithi of Shakti and, in Bhadrapada's dark fortnight, of Krishna's own birth.' Turns a lunar coincidence into a personal totem, in the same respectful-garnish register as the existing nakshatra deity/animal/tree cards.

ideas.title: Nearest festival to your birth date + 'how many Diwalis you've seen'
ideas.what: Show the major Hindu festival closest to the user's Gregorian DOB ('you were born 4 days after Diwali 1974') and a running count: 'you have lived through 52 Diwalis, 52 Holis, 76 Makar Sankrantis' (Sankranti is ~fixed 14 Jan so count = age; lunar ones = age, ~1 per year). Optionally 'the Diwali of your birth year fell on 13 Nov 1974.' Keys off the Gregorian DOB against a bundled festival-date dataset 1950-2010.
ideas.source: Bundled festival-date table 1950-2010. VERIFIED FULL DIWALI SERIES pulled from https://www.epicastrology.com/en/Festival/?name=diwali (e.g. 1950-11-09, 1974-11-13, 1985-11-12, 1999-11-07, 2005-11-01, 2010-11-05) and Holika Dahan from https://www.epicastrology.com/en/Festival/?name=holika_dahan (1950-03-03 ... 1970-03-22). Same site hosts per-festival 1950-2050 lists for Holi, Dussehra, Janmashtami, Ganesh Chaturthi, Navratri, Raksha Bandhan, Maha Shivratri; drikpanchang year-archive (https://www.drikpanchang.com/sitemaps/year/diwali/diwali-puja-calendar.html) is the cross-check.
ideas.feasible: MEDIUM (data-gathering, not code). Birth-time-free. Code is a trivial nearest-date search + subtraction. The work is assembling & double-sourcing the ~610-row table (10 festivals x 61 years). Recommend scrape epicastrology per-festival pages, cross-check a sample against drikpanchang, store as JSON keyed by year. Alternative (harder, riskier) is computing each festival live from the engine's (masa,paksha,tithi) rule — works but has adhika-masa / purnimanta-vs-amanta / drik-vs-suryasiddhanta edge cases that can shift a date by a day or a whole month in some years, so a verified static table is safer and matches the app's precompute ethos.
ideas.wow: 'You've watched 52 Diwalis go by. The very first — the one you were too small to remember — lit up the sky on 13 November 1974, just four days after you were born.' Concrete, warm, shareable; ties the abstract lunar calendar to a lived count.

ideas.title: Ritu (which of 6 seasons) + Ayana (Uttarayana/Dakshinayana) of your birth
ideas.what: Name the RITU (Vasanta/Grishma/Varsha/Sharad/Hemanta/Shishira) and the AYANA (Uttarayana = sun's northward half, Jan-Jun; Dakshinayana = southward, Jul-Dec) you were born in, each with a one-line poetic mood. Ritu from the engine's cal.Ritu (already computed) or from the birth masa; Ayana from the Sun's sidereal longitude the kundli already computes (Makara 270° through Mithuna 90° = Uttarayana; else Dakshinayana).
ideas.source: Ritu: engine cal.Ritu (name_en_UK ['Spring','Summer','Monsoon','Autumn','Pre-Winter','Winter'], verified node_modules/mhah-panchang/dist/mhah-panchang.esm.js:797-800) OR bundled 6-row masa->ritu table. Season/month mapping + ayana solstice logic verified: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ritu_(Indian_season). Ayana is a pure function of sunRashi/sun sidereal longitude already returned by rashiChart() in kundli.js.
ideas.feasible: TRIVIAL. Birth-time-free. Ritu is either already computed (read cal.Ritu) or a 6-row lookup on masa. Ayana is a one-line if-statement on the already-computed Sun longitude. ~0.5KB.
ideas.wow: 'You arrived in Sharad Ritu, autumn — skies washing clean after the monsoon, the season of Navratri and Diwali — during Dakshinayana, the sun's inward, contemplative southern march.' Poetic, ancient, and it costs almost nothing.

ideas.title: Your star's / tithi's festival day — nakshatras & tithis that ARE festivals
ideas.what: Several nakshatras and tithis recur annually as festivals; tell the user if theirs is one. E.g. Shravana nakshatra -> Onam & Raksha Bandhan vibe; Rohini -> Janmashtami's nakshatra (Krishna's star); Pushya -> Guru Pushya yoga (auspicious for buying gold); Chitra -> Chitra Purnima; Ashwini/Bharani etc. Plus tithi->festival from idea 3. 'The sky files your birthday under Rohini — the very star under which Krishna was born.'
ideas.source: Bundled small nakshatra->festival table (subset, ~8-10 rows) + the tithi->festival table. Rohini=Krishna's nakshatra and Shravana=Onam verified via festival-nakshatra searches; cross-check drikpanchang festival pages. Nakshatra already computed by kundli.js (moonNakshatra.index).
ideas.feasible: EASY. Birth-time-free. Pure lookup on the already-computed nakshatra index + tithi index. Only a handful of nakshatras map cleanly to a festival, so table is small and can be curated conservatively (only assert well-known links to stay in respectful-garnish register).
ideas.wow: 'Your janma-nakshatra is Rohini — the same star the sky was showing on the night Krishna was born.' Connects the user's totem-star (already a beloved beat) directly to living festival culture.

tables.name: yoga27 (27 Nitya Yogas -> nature + one-line meaning)
tables.keyedBy: ino index 0-26 matching engine cal.Yoga.ino (engine name_en_IN in parens must match exactly)
tables.sample: [0] Vishkambha (engine:'Vishkambha') — nature: mixed/inauspicious — 'the supporting pillar; steady but heavy, guard against over-effort.' | [2] Ayushman (engine:'Ayushman') — auspicious — 'long life and vitality; a yoga of health and endurance.' | [13] Harshana (engine:'Harshana') — auspicious — 'joy and thrill; wit, learning and delight.' | [16] Vyatipata (engine:'Vyatipata') — inauspicious — 'sudden upheaval; a day to avoid new beginnings.' | [26] Vaidhriti (engine:'Vaidhruthi') — inauspicious — 'pulling apart; scattered energy, better for reflection than action.' NOTE engine spellings: idx1='Prithi'(Priti), idx7='Dhrithi', idx10='Vridhi', idx26='Vaidhruthi'.

tables.name: karana11 (11 Karanas -> fixed/movable + deity + one-line)
tables.keyedBy: ino index 0-10 matching engine cal.Karna.ino (engine order: Bawa,Balava,Kaulava,Taitula,Garaja,Vanija,Vishti,Sakuna,Chatushpada,Nagava,Kimstughana)
tables.sample: [0] Bava (engine:'Bawa') — movable — 'auspicious for beginnings, permanent/creative works; a good-omen half-day.' | [2] Kaulava (engine:'Kaulava') — movable — 'friendship and social bonds.' | [6] Vishti/Bhadra (engine:'Vishti') — movable, INAUSPICIOUS — 'the Bhadra half; traditionally avoided for auspicious work.' | [7] Shakuni (engine:'Sakuna') — FIXED, deity Garuda — 'calm, intuitive, medicinal works.' | [10] Kimstughna (engine:'Kimstughana') — FIXED — 'the tail-end karana of new-moon; humble endings.'

tables.name: tithi15_festival (15 tithis -> presiding deity + festival if any)
tables.keyedBy: tithi ordinal 1-15 within a paksha (map engine tithi ino 0-14 = Shukla 1-15, 15-29 = Krishna 1-15; engine Amavasya at ino29, Punnami/Purnima at ino14)
tables.sample: Chaturthi(4) — deity Ganesha — 'Bhadrapada Shukla Chaturthi = Ganesh Chaturthi (Ganesha's birth).' | Ashtami(8) — deity Durga/Shakti & Kala-Bhairava — 'Bhadrapada Krishna Ashtami = Janmashtami (Krishna).' | Navami(9) — 'Chaitra Shukla Navami = Ram Navami (Rama's birth).' | Ekadashi(11) — deity Vishnu — 'fasting day, twice a month.' | Chaturdashi(14) — 'Phalguna Krishna Chaturdashi = Maha Shivratri.' | Purnima(15 Shukla) — 'full moon; Guru Purnima, Holi, Buddha/Hanuman.' | Amavasya(15 Krishna) — 'new moon; Kartika Amavasya = Diwali/Lakshmi; pitru/ancestor day.'

tables.name: ritu6 (6 seasons -> months + Gregorian + ayana + poetic mood)
tables.keyedBy: ritu index 0-5 (engine cal.Ritu name_en_UK) OR derived from masa pair
tables.sample: Vasanta/Spring — Chaitra+Vaishakha — Mar-Apr — Uttarayana — 'blossoming, renewal, the year's tender green start.' | Grishma/Summer — Jyeshtha+Ashadha — May-Jun — Uttarayana(ends at solstice) — 'peak heat and long light before the rains.' | Varsha/Monsoon — Shravana+Bhadrapada — Jul-Aug — Dakshinayana — 'the sky breaks open; green, fertile, romantic rain.' | Sharad/Autumn — Ashwin+Kartika — Sep-Nov — Dakshinayana — 'clarity after rain; the festival season, Navratri to Diwali.' | Hemanta/Pre-winter — Margashirsha+Pausha — Nov-Dec — Dakshinayana — 'crisp cooling, harvest calm.' | Shishira/Winter — Magha+Phalguna — Jan-Feb — Uttarayana(begins at solstice) — 'coldest, inward, the sun turns north again.'

tables.name: festivalDates1950_2010 (major Hindu festival Gregorian dates, bundled)
tables.sample: {year:1974, diwali:'1974-11-13', holi:'1974-03-08', dussehra:'1974-10-25', janmashtami:'1974-08-10', ganeshChaturthi:'1974-08-27', makarSankranti:'1974-01-14', mahaShivratri:'1974-02-21', rakshaBandhan:'1974-08-02', navratriStart:'1974-10-16'} | {year:1985, diwali:'1985-11-12'} | {year:1999, diwali:'1999-11-07'} | {year:2005, diwali:'2005-11-01'} | {year:2010, diwali:'2010-11-05'}. SOURCE: scrape per-festival pages at epicastrology.com/en/Festival/?name=<festival> (confirmed full 1950-2050 Gregorian lists for diwali & holika_dahan), cross-check sample years vs drikpanchang.com year-archive. Makar Sankranti is quasi-fixed (14 Jan, 15 Jan in some years) so can be near-computed. ~8KB JSON.
